next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

It had been two days since the outriders. Vel had put those two days to work. She took them off the main south track before sunset and kept them off it: through thigh-deep water that made Kem hiss through his teeth, over a scree shoulder where even goats would think twice, under deadfall so tangled he had to crawl on one elbow to keep from cracking the rib the hillside had given him. The first night they slept under an uprooted cedar with wet dirt against their backs and no heat at all. At dawn she sent him back half a mile to brush away the clean lordling tread he'd left in mud, then made him cross the stream barefoot because boots held prints in the leather. By midday the second day both of them smelled like cold water, leaf rot, and the iron note of his reopened palm.

Vel hadn't spent a point. She still had twenty-eight on the Ledger, enough if the Hoarded needed time to sort themselves out, enough if the next team still had to search instead of following a clean trail south. Kem hadn't spent either. That should have reassured her more than it did. He'd obeyed. He'd kept his hands off the deep end. But every so often the grin from the ruined clearing came back over his face before shame drove it off again, and she could not tell which one would win if he got scared the wrong way.

The cost had come from other places. His left heel was rubbed raw through the secondhand boot he'd traded into after abandoning the corridor pair. His cracked rib had stiffened from two cold nights and was punishing every breath he didn't bargain around. He'd torn the split in his...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...her lap and opened it by the last of the coals.

The earlier pages held mileage, wells, turns in the ridge, the names of settlements that no longer existed or had changed shape since the ink first dried. The later pages tightened. The handwriting there pressed harder, crowded closer, as if the writer had begun to understand that neatness was a luxury the south wouldn't reimburse. Weather notes thinned out. Supply notes disappeared. Distance stopped mattering. Reserve mattered. Timing mattered. The threshold pages had always looked wrong to Vel, less like travel instructions than an argument with a machine.

She ran a thumb over the scored paper where the older hand had dug in too hard.

The threshold notes all drove at the same ugly point: whatever reached the far end had to reach it with reserve left in one piece. She had enough for the road if nothing spectacular happened. She did not have enough to make the far end answer.

Two hundred and eighty-six.

The relief sat sour in her stomach.

Kem shifted in his sleep, one hand closing around the blanket edge, the other around nothing at all.

If he learned fast, if he stopped spending on fear every time fear changed clothes, if nobody from the Vault caught them first, he might still have enough when they reached the threshold.

Tomorrow meant slow miles, dry camp, and knocking the Glow out of his hand again if she had to.

Vel closed the journal and lay down without taking her hand off it.

By dawn he would still be at two hundred and eighty-six. If he wasn't, the road had already started eating what she came north to keep.
